question.text: Under the SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015, who qualifies as an "insider"?

answer.text: Only directors and key managerial personnel of a listed company
A connected person or any person who has or had access to unpublished price sensitive information (UPSI)
Only employees of the listed company who are directly involved in financial reporting
Any shareholder holding more than 1% of the paid-up capital of the company

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Under SEBI Insider Trading Regulations 2015, an "insider" is defined broadly as a connected person OR any person who has or had access to unpublished price sensitive information (UPSI). This definition extends well beyond directors and KMPs to include anyone with UPSI access.
**The Trap:** Limiting insiders to directors/KMPs or employees is a common misconception. Even an outsider (e.g., a lawyer, auditor, or investment adviser) who gains access to UPSI becomes an insider.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Insider trading concepts are tested across NISM Series VIII, X-A, X-B, and Series XV exams. The broad definition of "insider" is a frequently examined point.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** As an investment adviser, if you learn non-public material information about a client's holdings, you could be classified as an insider. Maintain Chinese walls and document information barriers.
